retrieved_docs.title: LangGraph: Stateful Agent Orchestration with Graphs
retrieved_docs.snippet:
LangGraph: Stateful Agent Orchestration with Graphs

LangGraph, built on top of LangChain, provides a graph-based abstraction for building reliable, stateful LLM agents. Developers define nodes (agent steps) and edges (transitions) in a directed graph, enabling cyclic workflows, human-in-the-loop checkpoints, and fine-grained state management. LangGraph is widely used for production-grade agentic pipelines requiring persistence and controllability.

retrieved_docs.title: Latent Space: The Rise of Agentic AI Platforms in 2025
retrieved_docs.snippet:
Latent Space: The Rise of Agentic AI Platforms in 2025

The Latent Space newsletter surveys the agentic AI platform landscape as of mid-2025. The analysis identifies three dominant approaches: (1) orchestration frameworks (CrewAI, AutoGen, LangGraph) focused on multi-agent coordination; (2) RAG-first platforms (LlamaIndex, Vertex AI Search) focused on grounding agents in enterprise data; and (3) evaluation-native platforms (Langfuse, Braintrust, PromptLayer) focused on reliability and measurement. The newsletter argues that evaluation will become the key differentiator as models commoditize.

retrieved_docs.title: Cognition Blog — 1
retrieved_docs.snippet:
Cognition Blog — 1

Cognition is expanding its APAC presence from Singapore, where enterprise demand, developer talent, and national AI adoption are converging. With Richard Spence leading the region, we’re helping teams across Asia-Pacific bring autonomous software engineering into production.
Mercedes-Benz is deploying Devin and Windsurf across its global engineering organization, starting with legacy modernization, cloud-native development, and logistics.
Start a Devin session in your terminal. When the work outgrows your laptop, hand the same session off to the cloud and keep going.
Building cloud agents is difficult. We share some learnings from building Devin.
10 months ago I argued against building multi-agent systems. Today, a narrower class works, where agents contribute intelligence while writes stay single-threaded. I want to share what we've learned building them at Cognition.
Local agents make you faster. Cloud agents do the work while you're not there.
Smaller specialized models can match or beat frontier generalists on the tasks they're trained for. Working with Applied Compute, we RL-trained SWE-check, a bug detection model that matches Opus 4.6 on our internal evals while running ~10x

retrieved_docs.title: Weaviate Blog — weaviate-agent-skills
retrieved_docs.snippet:
Weaviate Blog — weaviate-agent-skills

It has never been easier to build software. With tools like Claude Code, Cursor, and GitHub Copilot, we’ve moved into a high-velocity workflow often called ‘vibe coding.’ You describe a feature, an agent blueprints the logic, and the app comes to life.
But this speed usually hits a wall when it meets specialized infrastructure. While agents are great at general boilerplate, they struggle with the nuances of working with specialized tools. They often hallucinate legacy v3 Weaviate syntax, guess at hybrid search alpha parameters, or fail to implement efficient multivector embedding strategies. When you’re trying to manage three parallel agentic workflows, you don't have time to be a full-time debugger for an agent’s hallucinations. You need the implementation to be right on the first try.
To solve this, we are introducing Weaviate Agent Skills. This repository acts as a bridge between the most popular coding agents and Weaviate’s infrastructure.
Agent Skills has become one of the most popular tools to help coding agents write better code. The Agent Skills format, developed by Anthropic, works with Claude Code, Cursor, GitHub Copilot, VS Code, Gemini CLI, and many other tools. Our new

retrieved_docs.title: CrewAI: Role-Based Multi-Agent Orchestration
retrieved_docs.snippet:
CrewAI: Role-Based Multi-Agent Orchestration

CrewAI is an open-source framework that enables developers to build collaborative multi-agent systems where each agent has a defined role, goal, and backstory. Agents work together as a 'crew' to complete complex tasks through sequential or parallel workflows. CrewAI supports tool use, memory, and delegation, making it well-suited for automated research, content generation, and decision pipelines.

retrieved_docs.title: Qdrant Blog — case-study-deutsche-telekom
retrieved_docs.snippet:
Qdrant Blog — case-study-deutsche-telekom

How Deutsche Telekom Built a Multi-Agent Enterprise Platform Leveraging Qdrant
Manuel Meyer
·March 07, 2025
On this page:
How Deutsche Telekom Built a Scalable, Multi-Agent Enterprise Platform Leveraging Qdrant—Powering Over 2 Million Conversations Across Europe
Arun Joseph, who leads engineering and architecture for Deutsche Telekom’s AI Competence Center (AICC), faced a critical challenge: how do you efficiently and scalably deploy AI-powered assistants across a vast enterprise ecosystem? The goal was to deploy GenAI for customer sales and service operations to resolve customer queries faster across the 10 countries where Deutsche Telekom operates in Europe.
To achieve this, Telekom developed Frag Magenta OneBOT (Eng: Ask Magenta), a platform that includes chatbots and voice bots, built as a Platform as a Service (PaaS) to ensure scalability across Deutsche Telekom’s ten European subsidiaries.
“We knew from the start that we couldn’t just deploy RAG, tool calling, and workflows at scale without a platform-first approach,” Arun explains. “When I looked at the challenge, it looked a lot like a distributed systems and engineering challenge, not just an AI problem.”
Key Requirements

retrieved_docs.title: LangChain Blog — Deep Agents
retrieved_docs.snippet:
LangChain Blog — Deep Agents

Using an LLM to call tools in a loop is the simplest form of an agent. This architecture, however, can yield agents that are “shallow” and fail to plan and act over longer, more complex tasks. Applications like “Deep Research”, “Manus”, and “Claude Code” have gotten around this limitation by implementing a combination of four things: a planning tool, sub agents, access to a file system, and a detailed prompt.
Acknowledgements: this exploration was primarily inspired by Claude Code and reports of people using it for more than just coding. What about Claude Code made it general purpose, and could we abstract out and generalize those characteristics?
Deep agents in the wild
The dominant agent architecture to emerge is also the simplest: running in a loop, calling tools.
Doing this naively, however, leads to agents that are a bit shallow. “Shallow” here refers to the agents inability to plan over longer time horizons and do more complex tasks.
Research and coding have emerged as two areas where agents have been created that buck this trend. All of the major model providers have an agent for Deep Research and for “async” coding tasks. Many startups and customers are creating versions of

retrieved_docs.title: Microsoft AutoGen: Conversational Multi-Agent Framework
retrieved_docs.snippet:
Microsoft AutoGen: Conversational Multi-Agent Framework

AutoGen (by Microsoft Research) is a framework for building LLM applications where multiple agents communicate via structured conversations. It supports customizable agent roles, code execution, and tool use. AutoGen v0.4 introduces a layered architecture with Core, AgentChat, and Extensions, enabling modular and extensible agent systems for coding assistants, research automation, and enterprise workflows.

retrieved_docs.title: Tavily — about
retrieved_docs.snippet:
Tavily — about

/about us
The agentic web is vast and unstructured. Tavily delivers high-quality, fresh, and structured web context, making it easy for agents to stay grounded as they scale from prototypes to production.
/our mission
Our mission is to help AI agents navigate the web quickly and efficiently by shaping the context they see. We prioritize relevance, freshness, and efficiency so agents reason accurately without wasting tokens.
/team
We’re a team of collaborators, builders, and deep thinkers, obsessed with making reliable web access possible for AI agents in the real world. We move quickly, support each other, and stay focused on shipping systems that deliver real impact in production.

retrieved_docs.title: Decagon Blog — fine-tuning-ai-agents
retrieved_docs.snippet:
Decagon Blog — fine-tuning-ai-agents

Engineering fast, performant AI agents through fine-tuning
November 21, 2025
At Decagon, we don't rely on a single, monolithic model to power our agents. Instead, our architecture is built around a network of specialized models that work in coordination. Each is responsible for a distinct function, such as interpreting user intent, executing workflows, detecting hallucinations, or determining when to escalate to a human. This orchestration allows our agents to deliver precise, reliable, and controllable behavior, which a single large model can't guarantee on its own.
Great AI-driven experiences prioritize accuracy above all, because even a fast response is useless if it's wrong. But latency still matters, since the right answer must arrive quickly enough to keep the experience seamless and engaging. This is especially true for voice channels and other real-time applications, where every millisecond matters. Every component model in the agent architecture must be optimized for both performance and correctness.
Fine-tuning is critical to achieving that optimization. The process transforms general-purpose models into highly specialized experts that not only operate faster but also out
